events.text: We want to stick to the facts and be honest with Rhea about what the problem is and how we're addressing it.[br6.218][br6.299]Fifteen minutes later, Rhea's blood glucose level rose to 80 mg/dL, which was back inside of a safe range.[br13.937]

Once Rhea started to stabilize, we needed to report and document the error[br4.833][br4.914]and potentially also communicate with the provider or other people who are relevant because Vic was a student.[br10.826][br10.907]For details on how to report and document errors,[br13.802][br13.883]familiarize yourself with the policies and procedures of the facility where you work about incident or variance reports.[br20.063]

In our case, we needed to update the EHR with information about the drop in blood glucose,[br5.731][br5.812]the clear liquid carbohydrates, and how the blood glucose level changed afterward.[br10.713][br10.794]We also needed to directly contact Rhea's primary care provider and surgeon[br15.187][br15.268]to make sure they were aware of the incident.[br17.782][br17.863]Finally, we made sure to communicate to the next shift what happened so they could monitor Rhea as needed.[br23.272]

Afterward, when we had a calmer moment, I talked with Vic[br4.057][br4.138]and gave him some feedback on how to avoid errors like this in the future.[br8.34][br8.421]If you were in my position, what instruction would you give Vic?[br12.114]
